summary: Julia Evans describes her sudden appreciation for Docker after struggling to set up a complex C++ environment for neural network experiments. She needed Ubuntu 14.04 with many dependencies and was dreading reinstalling her OS until she remembered Docker could give her an isolated environment instantly. She highlights Docker's speed compared to VMs, the reproducibility of Dockerfiles, and the freedom to make messy configurations without risking her main system. The post captures the 'aha moment' of understanding Docker's practical value through a real problem.
key_points: Docker's killer feature is creating isolated environments for messy dependency chains without risking your host system
Docker containers start as fast as regular programs, unlike virtual machines which take forever
Dockerfiles make even chaotic environment setups reproducible — it's a 'reproducible mess'
The practical use case that clicked was needing Ubuntu 14.04 with C++ dependencies for neural network experiments
Docker's value becomes obvious when you have a real problem it solves, not from abstract explanations about containers
money_quotes: I didn't really understand what Docker was *for* until yesterday. I mean, containers. Cool. Whatever.
I am apparently past the age when that was fun.
I downloaded an Ubuntu 14.04 image, and suddenly I just had Ubuntu 14.04! And I could INSTALL THINGS ON IT. Without worrying!
It's not like a virtual machine which takes forever to start – starting a Docker container is just like, well, starting a program.
I don't have to worry about screwing up my computer while doing it, and then even if it's a mess, it's a *reproducible* mess!
The next time I need to compile a thing with horrible dependencies that I don't have on my computer and that conflict with everything, I'm totally using Docker.
key_insight: Docker's real value clicks when you need an isolated, reproducible environment for messy dependency chains — it turns an unreproducible mess into a reproducible one.
